coredata.dc:description: Winter jujube is prone to rapid ripening and deterioration during postharvest storage and transportation. This study developed a multifunctional chitosan (CS) composite film incorporating dialdehyde cellulose nanocrystal (DCNC) and carbon dots (CDs) derived from walnut green husks. DCNC functioned as cross-linking filler, while CDs served as active nanofillers. The CS composite film containing 3 wt% DCNC and 3 wt% CDs (CS/DCNC/CDs-3 %) exhibited significant improvements in UV-blocking, mechanical strength, gas barrier, and thermal stability, attributing to the formation of a dense film structure and synergistic effect of Schiff base and hydrogen bonding interactions among film components. Notably, this composite film demonstrated a high CO2/O2 selectivity of 14.1, facilitating the creation of an ideal atmosphere inside the package. This composite film exhibited remarkable antioxidant activity and displayed potent antibacterial capacity through photocatalytic ROS generation, achieving inhibition rates of 90.8 % against S. aureus and 86.8 % against E. coli. Additionally, this film showed favorable biosafety and biodegradability, addressing food safety and environmental concerns. Postharvest preservation tests confirmed that this film assisted by light exposure effectively regulated physiological metabolism and delayed the ripening of winter jujube, highlighting its potential as an active modified atmosphere packaging material for perishable fruit preservation.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and cellulose nanocrystal-incorporated chitosan composite films with enhanced gas selectivity and photodynamic antibacterial properties for fruit preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Winter jujube is prone to rapid ripening and deterioration during postharvest storage and transportation. This study developed a multifunctional chitosan (CS) composite film incorporating dialdehyde cellulose nanocrystal (DCNC) and carbon dots (CDs) derived from walnut green husks. DCNC functioned as cross-linking filler, while CDs served as active nanofillers. The CS composite film containing 3 wt% DCNC and 3 wt% CDs (CS/DCNC/CDs-3 %) exhibited significant improvements in UV-blocking, mechanical strength, gas barrier, and thermal stability, attributing to the formation of a dense film structure and synergistic effect of Schiff base and hydrogen bonding interactions among film components. Notably, this composite film demonstrated a high CO2/O2 selectivity of 14.1, facilitating the creation of an ideal atmosphere inside the package. This composite film exhibited remarkable antioxidant activity and displayed potent antibacterial capacity through photocatalytic ROS generation, achieving inhibition rates of 90.8 % against S. aureus and 86.8 % against E. coli. Additionally, this film showed favorable biosafety and biodegradability, addressing food safety and environmental concerns. Postharvest preservation tests confirmed that this film assisted by light exposure effectively regulated physiological metabolism and delayed the ripening of winter jujube, highlighting its potential as an active modified atmosphere packaging material for perishable fruit preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh fruits play a crucial role in a healthy diet by providing essential nutrients and vitamins that support bodily functions and disease resistance. Winter jujube (Ziziphus jujuba Mill. cv. Dongzao) is a widely cultivated fresh-eating fruit in China, highly favored by consumers for its crisp texture, sweet flavor, and rich nutritional profile. It is abundant in vitamins, minerals, dietary fiber, amino acids, and polyphenols, which contribute to its antioxidant properties, digestive benefits, immune enhancement, and cardiovascular protection (Dou et al., 2023). However, postharvest winter jujube is highly susceptible to water loss, nutrient depletion, and microbial contamination during storage and transportation. Additionally, due to its high respiratory metabolism, excessive CO2 accumulation within packaging often occurs, leading to anaerobic fermentation, softening, and browning, ultimately accelerating quality deterioration and reducing shelf life (Zhang et al., 2016). To mitigate these issues, the development of antibacterial films with high CO2/O2 selectivity is essential for effective winter jujube preservation. Conventional preservation methods primarily rely on microporous modified atmosphere packaging (MAP), which helps lower CO2 concentration inside the packaging. However, the high oxygen level inside microporous packaging compromises preservation efficacy of MAP (Cui et al., 2024). Furthermore, most microporous films are non-biodegradable petroleum-based plastics, exacerbating energy consumption and environmental pollution. In response to sustainability initiatives like the “plastic limit order” and “dual carbon” in China, bio-based food packaging films have emerged as promising alternatives to petroleum-derived plastics due to their biodegradability and renewability (Asgher et al., 2020).

Chitosan (CS), a cationic biopolymer derived from crustacean shells, is widely used in eco-friendly packaging due to its excellent film-forming ability, biocompatibility, and antibacterial properties (Jin et al., 2024). Research has shown that CS-based films can regulate CO2 and O2 permeability, thereby reducing the respiration rate of fresh produce (Kou et al., 2019). However, the industrial application of pure CS films in food packaging is limited by their weak mechanical properties and limited antibacterial and antioxidant capacity. Crosslinking is an appealing strategy to enhance the mechanical strength of CS films by forming highly entangled network structures. Common crosslinkers like glutaraldehyde (Liu et al., 2019), formaldehyde (Wei et al., 2024), and epoxy resin (Zhang et al., 2024) have been used, but their high toxicity to human health remains a major concern. As an alternative, cellulose—a naturally abundant polysaccharide—has emerged as an attractive reinforcement material due to its widespread availability, low cost, high surface hydroxyl content, and excellent biodegradability (Zhang et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023). Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC), derived from cellulose, can be further modified through periodate oxidation to introduce aldehyde groups, forming dialdehyde cellulose nanocrystal (DCNC). DCNC can then react with the amino groups of CS via Schiff base reactions, significantly enhancing the performance of the packaging materials (Ruan et al., 2022).

Incorporating functional fillers is another effective strategy for enhancing antibacterial properties of packaging films. Metal nanomaterials have been widely utilized in active food packaging due to their abundant active sites and high reactivity (Peng et al., 2024). For example, Li et al. (2024) developed antibacterial CS/sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel films by incorporating AgNPs. Liu et al. (2021) incorporated ZnO into the CS matrix to improve antibacterial property of the film. However, concerns regarding their potential migration into food and subsequent accumulation in the human body have prompted the search for safer alternatives. Certain plant polyphenols, such as tannic acid (Ma et al., 2021; Zhou, Ma, et al., 2021) and gallic acid (Rui et al., 2017), have been explored to enhance the antibacterial properties of food packaging. However, poor stability due to their susceptibility to oxidation, light, and heat, which can lead to the degradation of their beneficial properties over time. Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm, have recently garnered significant interest due to their low toxicity, ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, high stability, and exceptional antioxidant and photodynamic antibacterial activities (Ezati et al., 2022). CDs derived from renewable sources, are increasingly utilized in food packaging (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2023). Walnuts are cultivated globally for their economic, nutritional, and medicinal value. Walnut green husks (WGH), a major by-product of walnut processing, are often discarded as waste, contributing to environmental pollution and resource wastage (Xia et al., 2024). Notably, WGH contains approximately 50–60 % carbohydrates by dry weight, making it a promising raw material for the sustainable synthesis of CDs. Our preliminary experiment found that WGH contains 22.4 % cellulose, highlighting its potential for producing DCNC (Zhu et al., 2024). The utilization of WGH to simultaneously synthesize both CDs and DCNC presents a sustainable approach to valorizing agricultural by-products while reducing carbon emissions. Based on these considerations, we hypothesize that the performance of CS-based films would be enhanced by introducing DCNC and CDs. However, to the best of our knowledge, the combined effects of WGH-derived DCNC and CDs as nanofillers on CS-based packaging films, particularly in terms of gas selectivity and photodynamic antibacterial properties, remain unexplored and require further investigation.

This study aimed to develop a multifunctional CS-based film incorporating DCNC and CDs from WGH (Fig. 1 ). The influence of varying CDs content on the films' microstructure, mechanical strength, barrier performance, gas selectivity, and thermal stability was systematically examined. Additionally, the antioxidant and photodynamic antibacterial properties of films were evaluated. Furthermore, the practical application of the resultant film on winter jujube preservation was explored. This study provides novel insights into developing multifunctional bio-based films for perishable fruit preservation packaging.

WGH were collected from Dali walnut plantation (Yunnan, China). CS (molecular weight of 180 kDa, degree of deacetylation >95 %), sodium periodate (99 %), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazide (DPPH), and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CNC and DCNC derived from WGH
ce:section.ce:para: CNC from WGH was prepared, referring to our previous work (Liu et al., 2024). DCNC was synthesized using the periodate oxidation method outlined by Xiao et al. (2020), with the detailed procedure provided in Supplementary Materials. The oxidation degree was determined via sodium hydroxide titration described by Lee et al. (2020). The crystallinity of CNC and DCNC was measured based on our previous methodology (Liu et al., 2024).

The morphologies of CNC and DCNC were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100plus, JEOL, Japan). The chemical structures were characterized through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (NICOLET, iS50, USA) in a range of 4000–400 cm−1. The chemical composition was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, ThermoFisher, USA). The crystalline structure was analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Ultima, IV, Japan) in the 2θ range of 10° − 80°. The thermal stability was assessed with a thermal analyzer (METTLER, 1100LF, Switzerland) by incrementally increasing the temperature from 20 °C to 800 °C with a rate of 20 °C/min.

CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using WGH powder as the precursor. The detailed synthesis procedure is provided in Supplementary Materials. The microstructure of CDs was examined using TEM. UV–vis spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of CDs solution were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) and an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Germany), respectively. FTIR, XRD and XPS analyses of CDs were conducted using the same instruments as described in Section 2.2. Additionally, the antioxidant and antibacterial activities of CDs were evaluated, with detailed methodologies provided in Supplementary Materials.

CS-based composite films were prepared using the solution casting method. Briefly, 2 g of CS was dissolved in 100 mL of 2 % (v/v) acetic acid solution under continuous stirring at room temperature. Glycerol (0.6 g) was then incorporated as a plasticizer. The CS/DCNC film-forming solution (FFS) was formed by addition of varying content of DCNC (1, 3 and 5 wt% relative to CS) to the mixture and stirring for 1 h. The optimal DCNC content was determined to be 3 wt% by optimizing mechanical properties and water vapor barrier (see Supplementary Table S1). Subsequently, different content of CDs (1, 3, and 5 wt% relative to CS) were introduced, followed by 30 min of stirring and 10 min of ultrasonication to remove air bubbles. The FFS was then poured onto a 12 cm × 12 cm Teflon plate and air-dried at room temperature to form composite films, namely CS, CS/DCNC, CS/DCNC/CDs-1 %, CS/DCNC/CDs-3 %, and CS/DCNC/CDs-5 %. The films were conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % RH for two days before further testing and characterization.

The surface morphology of films was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, thermos, Scientific, Apreo, 2C, USA). The chemical structure and crystalline characteristics of films were examined by FTIR, XRD and XPS. Experimental section of the Supplementary Materials thoroughly outlines the testing procedures for the films' physicochemical properties, including optical, mechanical, water resistance, gas permeability, and thermal property.

The films' antioxidant capability was evaluated by scavenging DPPH and ABTS free radicals, as outlined by Zhang, Li, & Jiang (2020). The antibacterial ability of the films against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 29213) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were assessed using the plate counting method under natural light and dark conditions, as described by Sun et al. (2024). Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, including ·OH and ·O2 −, was detected using HTPA formation and DPBF quenching methods respectively (Yang et al., 2024). The HTPA method involves the conversion of non-fluorescent terephthalic acid (TPA) into fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA) upon reaction with ·OH, enhancing fluorescence signal at 430 nm. The DPBF quenching method relies on the oxidative bleaching of fluorescent DPBF by ·O2 −, converting it into non-fluorescent 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene, reducing fluorescence signal at 485 nm.
The biosafety of films was assessed through hemolytic activity and overall migration tests. Detailed testing procedures are provided in Supplementary Materials.

The biodegradability of films was evaluated under natural outdoor conditions according to Wang, Yang, et al. (2024). Briefly, film samples (2 cm × 2 cm) were buried 15 cm beneath the soil surface, with commercial polyethylene (PE) films serving as a control. The degradation process was monitored by photographing the samples every 15 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for fresh winter jujube preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh winter jujubes free of physical damage and insect infestation were purchased from local markets in Kunming, China. The jujubes were placed in polypropylene trays and packaged with either commercial PE film or CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film, while an unpackaged group served as the control. Each packaged group contained approximately 100 g of winter jujubes. All samples were stored at room temperature for 10 days. The jujubes packaged with CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film was stored under light and dark conditions, labeled as CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) and CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark), respectively. Visual appearance was photographed, physiological-biochemical and microbial properties were studied every two days throughout the storage period. Detailed testing methodologies are provided in Supplementary Materials.

Each test was performed at least three times, and the results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). To assess the significance of differences among the data, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was conducted following Duncan's multiple-range tests using SPSS 26.0 software (P < 0.05).

As shown in Fig. 2A, CNC exhibited a typical rod-like morphology with widths ranging from 4 to 13 nm and lengths from 37 to 332 nm. After oxidation, DCNC retained a similar rod-like structure (Fig. 2B), achieving an oxidation degree of approximately 43.62 %. The size of DCNC slightly decreased, with widths ranging from 3 to 8 nm and lengths from 24 to 267 nm, indicating that the oxidation treatment did not significantly alter the nanocellulose morphology (Xiao et al., 2020).

Fig. 2C presents the FTIR spectra of CNC and DCNC. Both samples exhibited characteristic cellulose absorption peaks at 3338, 2916, and 1032 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O − H, C − H, and C − O, respectively. Additionally, peaks at 1641 and 1369 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibrations of C − H and O − H (Lee et al., 2020). A distinct new absorption peak appeared at 1732 cm−1 in the DCNC spectrum, corresponding to the symmetric vibration of the aldehyde group. Meanwhile, an increased intensity of the bending vibration peak at 892 cm−1, associated with the hemiacetal group, was observed. These findings align with previous reports on periodate-modified CNC from bamboo (Kwak et al., 2020), confirming the successful preparation of DCNC.

As depicted in Fig. 2D, both CNC and DCNC exhibited characteristic peaks for C 1 s and O 1 s. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2E) revealed the presence of C–C/C–H (284.70 eV), CO (286.62 eV), and O–C–O (287.70 eV) bonds in both samples. Notably, a new peak at 289.13 eV (H–C=O) appeared in C1s of DCNC, further confirming the successful oxidation of CNC.

As displayed in Fig. 2F, typical cellulose I diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 15.5°, 22.6°, and 34.4° were observed for both CNC and DCNC, attributing to the (110), (200), and (004) planes, respectively (Xiao et al., 2020). This indicated that the crystalline structure of CNC remained unaltered even after oxidation. However, the crystallinity of DCNC (62.92 %) was slightly lower than that of CNC (74.66 %). This change is attributed to the oxidation-induced cleavage of pyranose rings, which disrupts the orderly molecular arrangement (El Miri et al., 2022).

As shown in Fig. 2G-H, both samples exhibited a three-stage thermal degradation pattern. The initial weight loss below 120 °C was due to moisture evaporation. The primary degradation stage occurred between 200 °C and 450 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of amorphous and partially crystalline cellulose structures (Y. Xu et al., 2023). The third stage, above 450 °C, was associated with cellulose carbonization and ash decomposition. The maximum degradation temperature (Tmax) during the primary decomposition phase was 293.4 °C for CNC and 294.7 °C for DCNC, with corresponding weight losses of 66.82 % and 62.95 %, respectively. These results indicate that DCNC exhibited slightly enhanced thermal stability compared to CNC.

The TEM image presented in Fig. 3A illustrates that the synthesized CDs exhibit a nearly spherical morphology with uniform distribution. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3B) revealed lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (Wang, Wang, et al., 2024). As displayed in Fig. 3C, the particle size of the CDs ranged from 1.04 to 3.89 nm, with an average diameter of 2.21 ± 0.58 nm. Due to their nanoscale dimensions and potential quantum confinement effects, the CDs solution appeared yellowish-brown under visible light and exhibited blue fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3D). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3D) displayed a prominent peak at 278 nm, attributed to n → π* transition of CO bonds (Parveen et al., 2024). Furthermore, the CDs exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission at 435 nm when excited at 320 nm. The emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths, shown in Fig. 3E, indicate a redshift in the emission peak with increasing excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity increased up to 320 nm and subsequently declined, demonstrating the characteristic excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs.

FTIR was employed to analyze the chemical groups present in the CDs (Fig. 3F). The peaks at 3379, 2934, 1708, 1602, and 1048 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O − H, C − H, CO, CC, and C − O, respectively. The peaks at 1402 and 1278 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibrations of O − H and C − OH (Khan et al., 2023). XRD pattern of the CDs (Fig. 3G) exhibited a broad peak at 2θ = 22.5°, indicating a disordered carbon structure with low crystallinity, possibly related to the presence of the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Wang, Wang, et al., 2024).

XPS analysis was performed to determine the chemical composition of the CDs (Fig. 3H). Three characteristic peaks at binding energies of 285.37 eV (C 1 s), 400.19 eV (N 1 s), and 532.25 eV (O 1 s) were detected. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3I) further revealed peaks at 284.69, 286.18, 288.04, and 292.74 eV, corresponding to C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N, CO, and O–C=O bonds, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3J) exhibited peaks at 399.80, 401.60, and 406.35 eV, assigning to C–N–C, NH, and NO bonds, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3K) displayed peaks at 532.40 eV and 530.96 eV, confirming the presence of CO and CO bonds (Yang et al., 2024).

The antioxidant activity of the CDs was evaluated using DPPH and ABTS assays. As illustrated in Fig. 3L, the radical scavenging efficiency increased with rising CDs concentration. At 200 μg/mL, the scavenging rates for DPPH and ABTS reached 85.83 % and 97.84 %, respectively. Notably, the CDs exhibited higher scavenging activity toward ABTS compared to DPPH, likely due to differences in the reaction systems—aqueous phase (ABTS) versus alcoholic phase (DPPH). The IC50 values for DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging were determined to be 108.61 μg/mL and 76.53 μg/mL, respectively. The antibacterial properties of the CDs were assessed via the agar-well diffusion method. Fig. 3M shows inhibition zone diameter of 4.48 mm for S. aureus and 3.95 mm for E. coli. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of CDs against S. aureus were 8 mg/mL and 12 mg/mL, respectively, whereas for E. coli, the MIC and MBC values were 14 mg/mL and 17 mg/mL, respectively.

The surface morphology of film was examined using SEM (Fig. 4 ). The pure CS film exhibited a smooth and uniform surface without visible pores or cracks. After incorporating DCNC into the CS matrix, no phase separation was observed, suggesting a high level of compatibility between the components. Although the CS/DCNC film showed a slight increase in surface roughness, it retained a relatively smooth texture, indicating a dense structure formed by the Schiff base cross-linking reaction between CS and DCNC (Fig. 5A). The addition of CDs led to a gradual increase in surface roughness and heterogeneity. However, when the CDs content was ≤3 wt%, the CS/DCNC/CDs film maintained a relatively smooth, uniform, and compact appearance, demonstrating that the CDs were well-dispersed within the CS matrix. This finding identifies 3 wt% as the optimal threshold for the uniform distribution of CDs in this study. Conversely, at a 5 wt% CDs content, aggregation occurred, forming visible clumps on the film surface. A similar phenomenon was reported by Khoshkalampour et al. (2023) when incorporating a higher CDs content into a gelatin matrix.

The FTIR spectra of the films are shown in Fig. 5B. The pure CS film exhibited distinct peaks at 3310 cm−1 (OH and NH stretching), 2932 cm−1 (CH stretching), 1650 cm−1 (amide I CO stretching), 1551 cm−1 (amide II NH bending), and 1020 cm−1 (C–O–C stretching). The spectra of CS/DCNC and CS/DCNC/CDs films were similar to that of pure CS film, except for a noticeable redshift of the OH peak to a lower wavenumber and a slight reduction in intensity. This shift suggests the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs, DCNC, and CS matrix (Fig. 5A). The anticipated CN stretching vibration from the Schiff base reaction between CS and DCNC was not detected, likely due to overlapping with the amide I peak of CS. A similar observation was reported in a study on fish gelatin films crosslinked with alginate dialdehyde (Sul et al., 2023).

The crystalline properties of the films were analyzed using XRD (Fig. 5C). The pure CS film exhibited diffraction peaks at 2θ = 8.1°, 11.2°, 15.6°, 17.8°, and 22.6°. The peaks at 8.1° and 11.2° corresponded to the hydrated crystalline structure, while the broad peaks at 17.8° and 22.6° reflected the amorphous structure of CS. The incorporation of DCNC significantly enhanced the peak intensities at 8.1° and 11.2°, whereas the intensity at 22.6° remained largely unchanged, suggesting improved crystalline ordering within the CS matrix. The addition of CDs to the CS/DCNC matrix caused a noticeable reduction in the intensity of the crystalline diffraction peaks, indicating that the introduction of CDs disrupted the crystalline state of the polymer matrix to some extent. This disruption likely resulted from hydrogen bonding interactions between the uniformly distributed CDs and the polymer matrix (Zhang et al., 2023). At a 5 wt% CDs content, most diffraction peaks became indistinguishable, likely due to CDs aggregation.

The chemical composition of the films was investigated using XPS (Fig. 5D). All the films exhibited three main peaks at 285.2 eV (C 1 s), 399.5 eV (N 1 s), and 532.3 eV (O 1 s). Fig. 5E-F display the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s and O 1 s for different films, respectively. For pure CS film, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.21 and 400.81 eV, corresponding to –NH2 and CN, respectively. For CS/DCNC film, a new chemical state in the N 1 s spectrum, attributed to CN, was observed, confirming Schiff base reaction between CS and DCNC. For CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film, the proportion of –NH2 decreased to 74.5 %, while that of CN increased to 22.6 %, which can be attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions between –NH2 of CS and –OH of CDs. The O 1 s spectrum of the CS film was fitted into two peaks at 531.02 eV (C=O) and 532.61 eV (C–OH). The proportion of C–OH decreased to 80.9 % in CS/DCNC film and further to 76.8 % in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film, further confirming hydrogen bonding interactions between –OH of CS and the oxygen-containing groups of DCNC and CDs.

The hue of packaging films plays a crucial role in conveying information about food quality and attracting consumer attention. The visual characteristics of the films are presented in Fig. 6A. The pure CS film exhibited high transparency, while the CS/DCNC film displayed a slight reduction in transparency due to the formation of Schiff base products. Incorporating CDs into the CS/DCNC matrix imparted a light yellow-brown hue to the films, attributed to the inherent color of CDs. The film color deepened as the CDs content increased. However, the films maintained visually acceptable transparency when the CDs content was ≤3 wt%, allowing the background to remain clearly visible through the films. The color parameters of the films are summarized in Table 1 . The integration of DCNC into the CS matrix resulted in a significant reduction in L* value (lightness) and an increase in both a* (redness) and b* (yellowness) values, indicating a shift toward a reddish-yellow hue. With increasing CDs content, L* value further decreased, while both a* and b* values significantly increased (P < 0.05). The ΔE (total color difference) value for CS/DCNC/CDs-5 % film showed a substantial increase from 2.88 ± 0.05 to 26.16 ± 0.60 (P < 0.05) compared to the pure CS film, highlighting the considerable impact of CDs on the ΔE of the composite films (Park et al., 2021).

The UV-blocking ability of packaging films is critical to prevent nutrient degradation, spoilage and discoloration caused by photooxidation. Fig. 6B presents the UV–Vis spectra of the films in the 200–800 nm range. The CS/DCNC composite film exhibited slightly lower transmittance than the pure CS film, likely due to the minor light-scattering effect of DCNC and the light absorption of CN in Schiff base product via n → π* transition. Incorporation of CDs further reduced the transmittance of the films across the entire wavelength range. To quantify UV-shielding capacity and transparency of films, transmittance values were recorded at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) (Table 1). The CS film exhibited the highest T280 (36.00 %) and T660 (87.60 %), indicating the poorest UV-blocking but the greatest transparency. Compared to the pure CS film, the CS/DCNC film showed a slight reduction in both T280 and T660, indicating improved UV-blocking with minimal transparency loss. With increasing CDs content, T280 drastically decreased to 0.03 %, while T660 declined to 64.20 % at 5 wt% CDs. These reductions can be attributed to CDs' strong UV absorption capacity. Similar UV-blocking effects were reported by Jayakumar et al. (2024) when CDs derived from lemon peel were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol-based films. These findings demonstrate that the incorporation of DCNC and CDs significantly enhances the UV-blocking performance of the films while maintaining acceptable transparency levels.

Packaging films with superior mechanical strength are essential to withstand external pressures and impacts during transportation and storage, thereby preventing damage to food products. Table 2 presents the thickness, tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and elastic modulus (EM) of various films. Incorporating DCNC and CDs into the CS matrix significantly increased film thickness (P < 0.05), likely due to the higher solid content. Compared to pure CS film, the CS/DCNC and CS/DCNC/CDs films exhibited notable enhancements in TS and EM, while EB value continuously decreased. These variations can be attributed to intermolecular cross-linking induced by the Schiff base reaction and strong hydrogen bonding interactions, which enhanced resistance to external stress and restricted the mobility of CS chains (Riahi et al., 2024). Notably, the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film demonstrated the highest TS (40.11 MPa) and EM (2152.31 MPa), along with a reduced EB (15.14 %). However, when the CDs content increased to 5 wt%, TS, EB, and EM values significantly declined. This reduction is likely due to CDs aggregation, leading to localized non-uniform regions that generate stress concentration, thereby weakening the film's mechanical properties. These findings are consistent with SEM observations. Similar results were reported by Wang et al. (2022), who found that excessive CDs content negatively impacted the mechanical properties of biopolymer films.

An effective water vapor barrier is essential for minimizing moisture exchange between food and surroundings, thereby preserving flavor and extending shelf life. Fig. 7A presents the water vapor permeability (WVP) values of different films. The pure CS film exhibited the highest WVP of 8.14 × 10−14 kg/m·Pa·s, attributed to its hydrophilic nature (Zhou, Tong, et al., 2021). Incorporating DCNC reduced the WVP to 7.06 × 10−14 kg/m·Pa·s, likely due to Schiff base crosslinking, which tightened the molecular structure and limited water vapor transmission pathways. The CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film exhibited the lowest WVP value of 5.64 × 10−14 kg/m·Pa·s. This enhancement is due to the uniform dispersion of CDs within polymer matrix, which facilitates the production of compact film structure via extensive hydrogen bonding among film components, thereby retarding the diffusion of water vapor through film (Xu et al., 2019). However, at 5 wt% CDs content, WVP increased due to increased free volume within films by CDs aggregation. Fig. 7B-C present the oxygen permeability (OP) and carbon dioxide permeability (CDP) of the films. The pure CS film exhibited the highest OP (4.38 × 10−15 kg/m·Pa·s) and CDP (3.64 × 10−14 kg/ m·Pa·s), indicating minimal resistance to O2 and CO2 permeation. The addition of DCNC and CDs significantly reduced OP and CDP values (P < 0.05), demonstrating improved gas barrier. The CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film showed 1.79 % and 2.51 % reduction in OP and CDP, respectively, compared to the pure CS film. However, increasing the CDs content to 5 wt% reversed these trends, consistent with the WVP findings. The enhanced O2 and CO2 barrier properties were primarily attributed to the compact film structure induced by hydrogen bonding among CS, DCNC, and CDs, which hindered gas permeability and diffusion through the films.

Maintaining optimal O2 and CO2 levels is crucial for regulating postharvest respiratory metabolism in fruits, thereby extending their storage life. The CO2/O2 selectivity of films is shown in Fig. 7D. The pure CS film exhibited the lowest CO2/O2 selectivity of 8.3, in agreement with Bourbon et al. (2011). The CO2/O2 selectivity of CS/DCNC film increased selectivity to 11.2, which further improved to a range from 12.8 to 14.1 after incorporating CDs. The highest CO2/O2 selectivity of 14.1 (improved by 69.43 % than pure CS film) was observed at 3 wt% CDs content. Generally, CO2/O2 selectivity correlates with the differences in gas dissolution and diffusion behaviors within the film (Hu et al., 2024). The presence of polar groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aldehyde) in the composite films provided numerous interaction sites for CO2, promoting its dissolution and diffusion, thereby accelerating CO2 permeability and enhancing gas selectivity. Moreover, Schiff base crosslinking and hydrogen bonding interactions modified the pore size distribution within the film, thereby regulating gas permeation selectivity. The smaller kinetic diameter of CO2 (0.33 nm) compared to O2 (0.34 nm) further facilitated its permeability. However, at 5 wt% CDs, aggregation reduced available interaction sites and created larger gas diffusion channels, resulting in a slight decline in selectivity. These findings demonstrate the synergistic action of DCNC and CDs to improve gas permeable selectivity and suggest that CO2/O2 selectivity can be controllable tuned by adjusting the CDs content. Compared with previously reported gas permselective bio-based packaging films, such as shellac-based film (4.7–8.6) (Zhou, Ma, et al., 2021), CS-PEG film (3.48–6.02) (Yan et al., 2022), methyl cellulose-starch-CNC film (1.67–2.63) (Sun et al., 2018), the composite films developed in this study demonstrated superior CO2/O2 selectivity, highlighting their potential for perishable fruit packaging.

Water resistance is critical for maintaining the structural integrity of packaging films, preventing damage caused by moisture absorption from high-moisture fruits. Fig. 7E-F present the water solubility (WS) and water contact angle (WCA) of the films, which are key indicators of water resistance. The pure CS film exhibited the highest WS (32.4 %) and the lowest WCA (81.2°), indicating strong hydrophilicity due to abundant amino and hydroxyl groups. Incorporating DCNC reduced WS to 26.6 % and increased WCA to 90.7°, owing to reduced hydrophilic group availability via Schiff base crosslinking. Additionally, increasing the CDs content further decreased WS while increasing WCA, reaching a minimum WS of 21.49 % and a maximum WCA of 96.7° for CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film. The enhanced water resistance is attributed to the formation of a dense film structure via extensive hydrogen bonding, which limited the interactions between hydrophilic groups and water. Similar trends have been previously documented in other studies, where lemon peel-derived CDs embedded in polyvinyl alcohol-based films (Jayakumar et al., 2024) and turmeric-derived CDs in CS-based films (Wen et al., 2023).

The thermal stability of the films was evaluated using TGA and DTG analyses, as shown in Fig. 8 . The thermal decomposition of all films occurred in three distinct stages. The initial phase, observed between 50 and 130 °C, corresponded to the evaporation of absorbed moisture. The second phase, spanning from 130 °C to 245 °C, was primarily attributed to thermal decomposition of glycerol (Yong et al., 2019). The third and most significant phase, occurring between 245 °C and 420 °C, corresponded to the decomposition of CS, DCNC, and CDs. In this phase, Tmax values for CS, CS/DCNC, and CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % films were 293.5 °C, 297.3 °C, and 300.6 °C, respectively, with corresponding weight losses of 35.82 %, 34.16 %, and 30.42 %. These results confirm that incorporating DCNC and CDs enhances thermal stability. This improvement is likely due to the interactions between DCNC, CDs, and CS matrix, which forms a Schiff base cross-linked network and hydrogen bonding, restricting molecular chain mobility and enhancing thermal resistance (Gao et al., 2021). However, at 5 wt% CDs content, Tmax decreased to 295.6 °C, which is attributed to aggregation effects that led to localized uneven thermal degradation.

Antioxidant packaging plays a vital role in mitigating oxidative stress damage during postharvest storage of fruits. Fig. 9 presents the DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities, along with the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values of different films. The results indicate that all films exhibited concentration-dependent antioxidant potential. The pure CS film displayed limited DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities due to the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups in its molecular structure (Abd El-Hack et al., 2020). The radical scavenging ability of the films improved upon incorporating DCNC, primarily due to Schiff base products reacting with free radicals (Park et al., 2021). Incorporating CDs into the composite films further enhanced their antioxidant activity, with efficacy increasing in proportion to the CDs content. The CS/DCNC/CDs-5 % film exhibited the highest radical scavenging activities, achieving 92.10 % for DPPH and 99.61 % for ABTS at a 10 mg/mL concentration. The IC50 values of the films significantly reduced (P < 0.05) with increasing CDs content. The CS/DCNC/CDs-5 % film demonstrated significantly lower IC50 values of 2.66 mg/mL for DPPH and 1.74 mg/mL for ABTS, compared to the pure CS film, which exhibited values of 7.73 mg/mL and 7.27 mg/mL, respectively. The enhanced antioxidant performance of CS/DCNC/CDs composite films is primarily attributed to CDs, which contain numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, that effectively capture free radicals and donate electrons or hydrogen atoms to neutralize them (Ji et al., 2019).

Antibacterial packaging films play a crucial role in inhibiting microbial growth within the packaging, thereby reducing foods spoilage. Fig. 10A-D illustrates the antibacterial effects of the films against S. aureus and E. coli. The pure CS film exhibited limited antibacterial activity, primarily due to its ability to disrupt bacterial cell membrane integrity and interact with intracellular nucleic acids and proteins (Li & Zhuang, 2020). The addition of DCNC enhanced the antibacterial properties of the CS/DCNC film, likely due to the Schiff base products formed between CS and DCNC (Cui et al., 2023). The incorporation of CDs, known for their photodynamic antibacterial properties, further improved the antibacterial effectiveness of the CS/DCNC/CDs films, with efficacy increasing in correlation with CDs content. Under natural light conditions, the CS/DCNC/CDs-5 % film exhibited inhibition rates of 97.56 % and 92.76 % against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. In contrast, under dark conditions, the inhibition rates for the same strains were significantly lower at 47.96 % and 39.96 %, respectively. Notably, the films displayed higher antibacterial activity against S. aureus compared to E. coli. This is attributed to the presence of an additional lipopolysaccharides layer in the E. coli cell wall, which forms a barrier that hinders the penetration of external antibacterial agents. Overall, the CS/DCNC/CDs composite films demonstrate excellent antibacterial properties under natural light exposure, underscoring their potential for use in active food packaging.

CDs can act as photocatalysts and generate ROS such as ·OH and·O2 − under light exposure (Riahi et al., 2022). ROS can cause oxidative damage to bacterial membranes, proteins, and nucleic acids, ultimately leading to bacterial cell death (Hong et al., 2024). To verify this mechanism, the ·OH and ·O2 − generation by the films through photocatalysis was measured (Fig. 10E-F). Under both light and dark conditions, the fluorescence intensities of HTPA and DPBF for CS and CS/DCNC films remained stable, indicating the absence of ROS generation. However, when CDs-containing films were exposed to light, the fluorescence intensity of HTPA rapidly increased, while the fluorescence signal of DPBF significantly decreased with prolonged light irradiation, confirming the generation of ·OH and ·O2 −. Conversely, under dark conditions, the fluorescence intensities of HTPA and DPBF for CDs-containing films showed negligible changes, indicating no ROS generation. These findings confirm that CDs-containing composite films effectively produce ROS under light exposure, providing a support for above photodynamic antibacterial activity and a basis for subsequent preservation packaging application.

Assessing the biosafety of food packaging films is essential due to their direct contact with foodstuff. In this study, the biosafety of the films was characterized through hemolytic activity and overall migration (OM). The hemolytic activity results are presented in Fig. S1A, where water and PBS buffer were used as positive and negative controls, respectively. All films exhibited hemolysis ratios below 5 %, complying with ASTM standards, which confirms their favorable hemocompatibility. The OM values of the films in different food simulants are shown in Fig. S1B. Distilled water, 50 % v/v ethanol, 3 % w/v acetic acid, and n-heptane were employed to represent aqueous, alcoholic, acidic, and fatty food simulants, respectively. Among all the tested conditions, the films exhibited the lowest OM values in distilled water and the highest in n-heptane. This trend is likely due to the nonpolar nature of n-heptane, which enhances film swelling and leads to a looser structure that facilitates the dissolution of film components (Guazzotti et al., 2022). The incorporation of DCNC and CDs effectively reduced the migration rate of the films. Notably, the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film exhibited the lowest OM values across all simulants compared to the pure CS film, showing reductions of 55.04 % in water, 53.97 % in 50 % ethanol, 39.33 % in 3 % acetic acid, and 33.37 % in n-heptane. This reduction can be attributed to the compact film structure that impeded the permeation of simulants. Overall, all films kept OM values below the regulatory limit of 10 mg/dm2, confirming their suitability for food packaging applications. However, while these films have passed in vitro safety assessments, they must still comply with various regulatory requirements before being approved for food packaging use.

The biodegradability of the films was evaluated by burying them in natural soil to simulate real environmental conditions, with commercial PE film used as a reference. As illustrated in Fig. S1C, all as-prepared films exhibited approximately 50 % degradation after 15 days, with partial remnants visible after 30 days, and complete decomposition by day 45. Interestingly, films containing DCNC and CDs degraded more slowly than the pure CS film, likely due to their improved water resistance and antibacterial properties, which limited water penetration and microbial growth in soil, thereby delaying degradation (Geng et al., 2024). In contrast, the PE film showed no visible signs of degradation throughout the testing period. These findings suggest that CS/DCNC/CDs films are environmentally friendly and biodegradable, offering a sustainable alternative for food packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for fresh winter jujube preservation
ce:section.ce:para: Based on its superior physicochemical and functional performance, the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film was selected for packaging winter jujubes. Fig. 11 illustrates the changes in appearance, physiological-biochemical and microbial indexes of winter jujubes over a 10-day storage period at room temperature.

Color change acts as the direct ripeness indicator of winter jujubes, gradually transitioning from green to reddish-brown. As illustrated in Fig. 11A, both the control and PE-packed winter jujubes exhibited progressive color changes, shifting from green to partially red and eventually turning entirely reddish-brown, indicating increased ripening. After 10 days of storage, jujubes in the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group retained a desirable color, with only slight reddening, whereas the majority turned red in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) group. Cross-sectional analysis revealed that unpackaged jujubes started wrinkling by day 6, with large areas of flesh browning, and became severely shriveled by day 10. Winter jujubes packed with PE film also were obvious dehydrated by day 8, along with visible internal flesh browning and decay spots. Conversely, jujubes in the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group maintained a relatively plump shape, with good flesh color and texture, while signs of decay were appeared in the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) group. These observations suggest the functional film effectively delayed the ripening and aging of winter jujubes under light condition.

Respiration intensity, which reflects metabolic activity and fruit maturity, was monitored throughout the storage period (Fig. 11B). All groups exhibited an initial increase in respiration intensity, followed by a decline. The control group demonstrated the highest respiration intensity, whereas the PE group displayed the lowest, likely due to its strong barrier properties that restricted oxygen supply and reduced moisture evaporation. However, the PE packaging rapidly created a high-CO2 and high-humidity atmosphere, which may induce anaerobic respiration and microbial proliferation, thereby accelerating fruit spoilage. In contrast, the respiration intensity in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group decreased moderately. This reduction can be attributed to the superior CO2/O2 selectivity of the composite film, which helps maintain appropriate CO2 and O2 levels within the packaging, thereby regulating respiration. Additionally, the respiration intensity in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) group was slightly higher than in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group, likely due to the consumption of oxygen by photochemically generated ROS, which further slowed respiration in the light-exposed group.

As illustrated in Fig. 11C, all jujubes consistently lost weight during storage, attributed to transpiration and respiration (Geng et al., 2023). Generally, fruit weight loss is significantly influenced by the water vapor barrier properties of packaging, with stronger barriers resulting in reduced weight loss. By the end of storage, the control group experienced the highest weight loss (32.21 %) due to a lack of protective packaging, whereas PE group exhibited the lowest weight loss (14.11 %) due to low water vapor permeability of PE film. The CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group showed a slower weight loss trend compared to control group, though slightly higher than PE group. This is attributed to the effective water vapor barrier of the film, which prevented moisture loss, as well as its favorable CO2/O2 selectivity, which reduced respiratory metabolism. The slightly higher weight loss observed in the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) group is likely due to higher respiration intensity in the absence of light, as previously discussed.

Firmness, which affects the fruit's taste and texture, decreased in all groups over the storage period due to the degradation of cell wall components like pectin and cellulose by pectinase and cellulase (Fig. 11D). The PE-packed jujubes exhibited the most significant loss of firmness, with a final firmness value of 2.29 N on day 10. This is likely due to the high impermeability of PE film, which led to H2O condensation inside the package, promoting excessive microbial proliferation and accelerating the softening and decay of jujubes. On day 8, unpackaged jujubes exhibited a slight increase in firmness due to significant moisture loss. In contrast, CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group exhibited the slowest decline in firmness, maintaining levels 21.92 % and 61.54 % higher than those of the control and PE groups, respectively, by day 10. Under dark, the firmness of jujubes decreased more rapidly, likely due to the lack of the photocatalytic antibacterial effect, which led to partial spoilage of jujubes. The appropriate barrier properties and effective photodynamic antibacterial activity of the functional film played a crucial role in mitigating softening and decay of jujubes.

TSS content is an important indicator of fruit quality and ripeness. Fig. 11E illustrates the changes in TSS levels among different groups during storage. The TSS values in control, PE, and CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) groups initially increased before declining. The initial increase in TSS was attributed to the hydrolysis of insoluble polysaccharides into monosaccharides (Liu et al., 2023), whereas the subsequent decrease was due to fruit aging and decay. In contrast, CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group exhibited a consistently stable increase in TSS, indicating a more favorable physiological state and a delayed ripening process. By day 10, the TSS content increased to 14.23 %, significantly higher than that of the other groups. Interesting, the control group maintained the highest TSS levels throughout storage, likely due to greater water loss, which resulted in a relatively higher concentration of soluble solids.

Vitamin C, a key antioxidant in winter jujube, is highly susceptible to oxidation, decomposition, and depletion during storage. As shown in Fig. 11F, the vitamin C content in all groups followed a trend of initially increasing before subsequently declining. From day 0 to day 6, vitamin C content increased due to nutrient conversion through respiratory metabolism (Zheng et al., 2022). In the later storage period, vitamin C levels declined sharply due to over-ripening and senescence. By day 10, vitamin C levels significantly decreased to 239.18 mg/100 g in control group, 215.66 mg/100 g in PE group, and 271.68 mg/100 g in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) group, indicating considerable nutrient loss. In contrast, CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group consistently retained the highest vitamin C content throughout storage, with a final value of 309.73 mg/100 g. This is likely attributed to the superior oxygen barrier properties, antioxidant and photocatalytic antibacterial effects of the composite film, which effectively delayed vitamin C decomposition and consumption.

As depicted in Fig. 11G, the initial TBC value of winter jujube was 2.24 log CFU/g, with all groups exhibiting a continuous increase in TBC during storage. By day 10, TBC values reached 7.10 log CFU/g in control group, 7.62 log CFU/g in PE group, and 6.51 log CFU/g in CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Dark) group. Notably, TBC in PE group exhibited the fastest growth rate from day 6, primarily due to the high-humidity environment within the packaging, which favored bacterial proliferation. The CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % (Light) group demonstrated the slowest bacterial growth, reaching only 4.59 log CFU/g by day 10. This significant improvement is mainly attributed to the outstanding photocatalytic antibacterial properties of the composite film. In summary, above findings demonstrate that the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film with light exposure effectively prolonged shelf life of winter jujubes to 10 days at room temperature.

In summary, this study successfully developed CS-based packaging films incorporating WGH-derived DCNC and CDs. The DCNC and CDs were used as crosslinking and functional fillers, respectively. The effects of varying CDs content on the physicochemical and functional properties of the films were systematically investigated. Characterization from SEM, FTIR, XRD, and XPS demonstrated excellent compatibility among the components and confirmed the formation of a dense cross-linked film structure through Schiff base and hydrogen bonding interactions. The superior performance of the CS/DCNC/CDs composite film, compared to pure CS and CS/DCNC films, highlights the synergistic effect of DCNC and CDs in enhancing the properties of CS-based films. Specifically, the CS/DCNC/CDs film exhibited significantly improved UV-blocking, mechanical strength, gas barrier, and thermal stability. These enhancements directly contribute to the improved protection of perishable products by reducing UV-induced degradation, limiting gas exchange, and maintaining the structural integrity of the packaging during storage. Notably, the CS/DCNC/CDs-3 % film demonstrated a higher CO2/O2 selectivity (14.1), which is crucial for regulating respiration in fresh fruit, thereby slowing ripening and extending shelf life. Additionally, its outstanding antioxidant and photodynamic antibacterial properties effectively mitigated oxidative stress and inhibited microbial growth, further preserving fruit freshness during storage. Furthermore, the composite films complied with ASTM standards for hemolysis rate (<5 %), maintained total migration values below the legal limit (10 mg/dm2), and exhibited complete degradation in soil, confirming their excellent biosafety and biodegradability. The functional film with light exposure effectively preserved the post-harvest quality of winter jujubes, significantly extending their shelf life to 10 days at room temperature. This study offers a sustainable solution to mitigate environmental pollution by repurposing walnut by-products into eco-friendly food packaging materials and opens an avenue for developing multifunctional bio-based packaging materials for perishable fruits. Moreover, these findings provide a new perspective for researchers in fields requiring controlled atmosphere applications, such as pharmaceuticals and medical supplies, further expanding the potential applications of this innovative packaging material.

Bingzhen Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Kun Wang: Validation, Investigation. Fangfei Sun: Software, Data curation. Yongliang Zhuang: Resources. Hui Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lincai Peng: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
